data.title: Nvidia Teed Up For A Strong Q3 Earnings Report (Earnings Preview)
data.description:
Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) is set to report Q3 ’26 earnings on November 19, 2025 after market close. Despite the recent headlines of SoftBank (OTCPK:SFTBY) selling its entire $5.83b stake in NVDA shares, I have reason to believe that Nvidia is set up to report significant growth with a durable growth trajectory for the final quarter of eFY26. Given the optimistic outlook, I am reiterating my Strong Buy rating with a price target of $351/share at 26.62x eFY27 price/sales.

You can review my previous coverage of Nvidia here:

“Nvidia Is Still In The Early Innings Of The AI Revolution.”

Nvidia Earnings Expectations

Nvidia received significant support during the calendar year calendar year Q3 ’25 earnings cycle, particularly driven by the major hyperscalers. The biggest takeaway received from the recent earnings reports was the increased spend for compute capacity going into the end of 2025 and into 2026. In total, the 3 major cloud services providers [CSPs], including Microsoft (MSFT), Alphabet (GOOG), and Amazon (AMZN) invested an aggregate $267b in the last twelve months, largely directed towards data center, network infrastructure, and compute capacity [CPUs & GPUs].

  • Microsoft: $78b estimated capital spend for eCY25 [fiscal year q3’25-q2’26].
  • Alphabet: $92.6b estimated capital spend for eFY25.
  • Amazon: $125.8b estimated capital spend for eFY25.
  • Meta Platforms: $71.85b estimated capital spend for eFY25.

Total capital outlay: $368.25b.

In addition to this, management outlined increased capital deployment for 2026, largely for sourcing additional compute capacity to support the heightened demand for cloud services.

With accelerating demand and a growing RPO balance, we're increasing our spend on GPUs and CPUs. Therefore, total spend will increase sequentially, and we now expect the FY '26 growth rate to be higher than FY '25.

Amy Hood, EVP & CFO, Microsoft.

You're going to see us continue to be very aggressive investing in capacity because we see the demand. As fast as we're adding capacity right now, we're monetizing it. It's still quite early and represents an unusual opportunity for customers in AWS.

Andrew Jassy, President, CEO, & Director, Amazon.

We're continuing to invest aggressively due to the demand we're experiencing from Cloud customers as well as the growth opportunities we see across the company… Looking out to 2026, we expect a significant increase in CapEx.

Anat Ashkenazi, SVP & CFO, Alphabet.

Granted, investors must bear in mind that not all capital deployed will go towards Nvidia GPUs, particularly as Alphabet and Amazon will leverage their internally developed chips to build out additional capacity. Specifically, Alphabet is expecting to utilize its Ironwood TPUs to build out its 1GW data center as part of a partnership with Anthropic (ANTHRO). Amazon, in a similar fashion, will be deploying upwards of 1mm Trainium2 chips for the data center developed in partnership with Anthropic.

Net of these two major buildouts, I have reason to believe Nvidia’s GPUs will remain as a critical component for new AI infrastructure. An additional factor investors must consider is that the hyperscalers may be entering or nearing a refresh cycle for the first generation of AI infrastructure capacity in the coming year. Management at Microsoft hinted in the Q1 ’26 earnings call that aging GPUs will need to be replaced. Investors must bear in mind that OpenAI launched ChatGPT at the end of November 2023, a mere 2 years ago. With data center GPUs expected to run for 1-3 years, the CSPs are likely entering into the first refresh cycle as it pertains to the AI business. With this in mind, I believe that the CSPs will not only need to invest in growth capital but also begin investing in maintenance capital for AI infrastructure, potentially compounding the investment outlay for the coming years.

Supporting growth investments is the cumulative backlog of $1.21T across the CSPs, including Oracle Corp. (ORCL). Investors should bear in mind that this is loosely tied to AI infrastructure as cloud hosting services will also cover general compute capacity. Nonetheless, the growing backlog supports the investment outlay for adding compute capacity at an increasing rate for the next fiscal year.

In terms of Nvidia’s business in China, Colette Kress, EVP & CFO of Nvidia, indicated at the Goldman Sachs Communacopia + Technology conference 2025 that Nvidia has received licenses for several customers in China to ship H20 GPUs. On November 11, 2025, the Wall Street Journal reported that Chinese chip production may be facing certain setbacks that may solidify Nvidia’s dominance in the market. One of the factors included the challenges Chinese foundries face with less advanced manufacturing systems producing lower quality chips.

In the report, Morgan Stanley analysts noted that manufacturing Huawei’s 910C chips using SMIC’s technology may result in only 5% of chips being of quality. The WSJ report also indicated that engineers in China are facing challenges using alternative chips to Nvidia’s technology, including “overheating, system crashes, and lack of software support.” This may be a positive sign for Nvidia re-ramping up business in the country following the trade restrictions for Chinese sales.

Nvidia Financial Outlook

Looking to Q3 ’26, I’m forecasting Nvidia to generate $58b in net revenue with an adjusted EPS of $1.33/share. I incrementally raised my revenue guidance from my previous report covering Nvidia given the recent Q3 ’25 earnings report by Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), which presented stronger Gaming & Client segment revenue for the quarter. I believe this should translate to strength in the segment for Nvidia, despite being a relatively smaller market segment when taking into consideration the size of the Data Center segment.

Given the updated guidance for selling to the Chinese market, I believe Nvidia may realize stronger revenue for eFY27 as the firm ramps up production of the H20 architecture.

Risks Related to Nvidia

Bull Case

Nvidia remains the leader for AI accelerators, posing a dominant position in both the U.S. and China [in terms of technology, as suggested by the report in the WSJ]. The investment cycle for compute capacity remains durable, particularly as a refresh cycle may be imminent.

Bear Case

The hyperscalers are heavily investing in custom ASICs, which are expected to be deployed for Anthropic’s data center capacity.

NVDA shares are currently trading at a relatively rich premium at 28.79x price/sales, potentially exposing investors to certain risks in the upcoming quarter. If management’s outlook for Q4 ’26 is relatively lackluster, shares may experience a significant decline.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

November 21, 2025 options trading on NVDA shares is showing some hedging going into the earnings call with significant puts volumes being traded at $185, potentially suggesting investors are anticipating a pullback in the share price.

Despite options positions, I believe NVDA shares are trading somewhat neutral as investors await the Q3 ’25 earnings call on November 19th.

Fundamentally, I believe NVDA shares should be valued at $351/share at 26.62x eFY27 price/sales, the target scenario in the valuation table below. Given the strong catalysts at hand, I believe Nvidia will be in a position to realize continued top-line growth for the coming years, particularly as the hyperscalers build out large-scale AI data centers to support the growing demand. I am reiterating my Strong Buy rating for NVDA shares.

Using the model: the valuation table above references my financial forecast in the firm’s “financial position” section and ties it to the stock’s historical trading premiums. The trading premium array is derived through the normal operating cycle with the blue-sky scenario being the stock’s peak multiple and the gray-sky scenario being the lowest point. The target multiple aims for the midpoint, or the most likely trading range for the company’s stock. The trading multiples from there are set to a probability factor based on the likelihood of the stock trading at that premium based on its historical presence. From there, the trading multiple is tied to the probability factor to derive its relative market cap and relative multiple.

data.title: SSK: When Staking Drives Dividends
data.description:
Introduction

The SSK REX-Osprey SOL + Staking ETF (SSK), is an option for those seeking exposure to the Solana cryptocurrency and at the same time obtain monthly dividends derived from staking.

Next, the main thread of the article will be to show in such a way that the reader understands what staking is and how the fund generates cash to distribute monthly through this methodology. Similarly, we will compare it with other market options and we will finish by analyzing the current correction phase.

Holdings: Know What You Own

Currently the fund has three Solana formats in its portfolio: firstly, 53.15% are holdings of the currency, 43.86% correspond to CoinShares which are acquired shares of CoinShares Physical Staked Solana ETP (SLNC) , 5.16% correspond to First American Government Obligs and finally, the fourth way (2.85%) is LSD (Liquid Staking Derivative) from JitoSOL.

This is important because the fund has 3 different ways to obtain returns through staking. But, before delving into how it is generated, let's see what staking is.

Staking & Validators

If you are not familiar with the blockchain world and without entering into technicalities or vocabulary “on-chain” staking consists of a simple way, to deposit an asset for a flexible (can be withdrawn at any time) or fixed term (there is a commitment of time for which it must remain deposited) and in return, receive an interest receive interest in the same asset (i.e., Petar deposits 10 solanas on Platform B and commits to maintain them for two years, in exchange, he receives X% of solanas Y every time). Such operations are very common in the DeFi industry.

As additional information, the concept of staking was born with networks with protocol PoS (Proof Of Stake) in which its main characteristic is that validators, responsible for processing transactions and critical part in the security and operation of a blockchain, have the obligation or requirement to deposit for a period of time Y (renewable) an X amount of the native cryptocurrency in staking to be able to exercise their functions.

However, it remarkable to highlight an important nuance: this total amount X is not only the validator’s own stake and it also includes delegated stake, but it (the “delegated part”) must be added and this is where portfolio management becomes critical: it hinges on the routes the fund takes to extract yield from SOL. Depending on the setup, the manager may select validators manually or rely on an automated selection/rebalancing mechanism, but we’ll discuss this process later.

In reward for the operational work of validating and processing transactions and for locking their coins/tokens, they receive a certain amount of the native currency for each transaction processed. Add that not only does this type of deposits exist, but in addition to the staking of validators, within each blockchain protocol, other ways of staking, lending, farming, liquid staking, etc. have been developed. In what is known as the world of decentralized finance: DeFi.

Without going too far into technicalities, delegation (which the fund does with part of its SOL) is a mechanism through which any entity that has solanas in its possession can interact with this program to participate in the staking, as if it were another validator. This is what the vehicle does directly with 2.85% of its total assets and indirectly with 43.86% (since CoinShares runs its own delegation mechanisms). In this way, validators also compete with each other to provide attractive conditions for raising capital. Here I leave you a list of the main pools of liquidity by TVL (Total Value Lock), where you can see that Jito is the largest and most liquid. List of pools by market cap:

Now that we have a better understanding about what staking is and also the figure of the validator, let's continue to delve into the ways through which the ETF seeks to generate a monthly dividend. Although we have previously described, there would be three ways in which it could generate the stake, we will only describe two ways since two of them are integrated and this is how we will treat them.

1) CoinShares Physical Staked Solana

At the moment, the vehicle has in its holdings almost 43% of shares in the ETP of Solana de CoinShares. Here the operation is simple, CoinShares offers a fixed 3% per year that distributes daily in exchange for keeping all the extra returns and applying a management fee of 0%. In this way, each day the number of shares held by the fund is the same and there is a daily increase in the Coin Entitlement (CE), that is, the amount of SOL backed by securities increases.

This approach is a straightforward way to secure a 3% distribution with low operational burden, especially when the issuer does not charge any management fee. You give up some of the potential performance you might otherwise get in exchange for simplifying operational management and reducing risk (uncertainty).

2) Solana , LSD (Liquid Staking Derivative) of JitoSOL & Native Staking

In its prospectus, it is stated that the fund does not have the permissions to be able to make any type of on-chain operation with its holdings. In this way, the operation is limited to:

  1. Buy SOL

  2. Buy SOL ETPs/ETFs

  3. Execute Native Staking: delegate directly to validators

  4. Execute Liquid Staking via:

    • Multi-validator

    • Per-validator

Likewise, it cannot access all types of liquid staking that exist (point 4) and therefore these types of operations are limited to multi-validator and pools per validator (aggregators) liquidity pools.

So why do we put the solanas part together with the LSD holdings? Because really, LSD is one of the ways that the fund has to move the solanas to generate passive returns. Therefore, solanas are the way to access this type of operations and generate rewards that are transformed into dividends.

Currently the fund is using the second (described in the previous section) and the fourth multi-validator way, to generate performance. However, although we would like to describe and delve into each of the ways, the objective of this article is to understand in the simplest way the mechanisms and risks associated with these operations. That is why we will make a brief general comment and we will rely on a more visual comparative table that we will also comment on.

In any of the three ways (the fourth grouping two sub-tracks), the fund can access these staking services. In the table, each column refers to each path to generate passive revenue, while each row is a criterion (to assess). Likewise, the aspects have been categorized using a simple rule from 1 to 3 being 3 the best (green) and 1 the worst (red), for a more visual comparison.

In this way, the color of the last two rows of the table is based on the sum of the values. However, the penultimate row also adds a weighting component on the aspects based on the interests of the fund, giving greater weight to liquidity and ease in operational management.

Currently it is using the Multi-Validator LST, which from our point of view is the best option for the fund because it simplifies operational management, the liquidity of the position is high and the mechanism automatically rebalances internally to consistently provide the best available conditions and although theoretically it does not keep in possession solanas, the synthetic they receive as a counterpart is safe and very liquid. Through the Native Staking route, it improves mainly the security and diversification potential but with a heavier operation, while the Per-Validator LST route improves mainly on the liquidity part but not too much with respect to the Multi-Validator.

SSK ETF vs Others

Compared to other options such as SOLZ, GSOL or BSOL, SSK ranks high by expense ratio at 0.75% vs 0.20% of BSOL or 0.00% of GSOL (in the future it will be 0.35%) and only below SOLZ which charges a fee of 0.95%. If you are not seeking recurring distributions/income, today GSOL and BSOL are positioned as the most competitive options.

However, in the event that in addition to being exposed to the cryptocurrency SOLANA wants to receive a monthly income for it, SSK is the winning option with a higher dividend yield of 2.07% vs 1.03% from SOLZ. However, it should be noted that SSK has only only ~3 months of history, so the trailing 12-month (TTM) distribution rate may be under or overstated. As a final note, in both cases the distribution is not guaranteed and they ensure that they can vary significantly month to month and be equal to zero. It is also important to note that SOLZ is futures-based, whereas SSK buys spot SOL, which reduces financing costs and improves NAV formation/price discovery.

Solana Technicals

On the top graph, we have Solana's performance on a weekly basis. October has been a particularly bearish month for the entire crypto ecosystem. the question we ask is whether this correction is going to continue or the market is capitulating.

The shaded area 160$-140$ approx. has been an area that has served both resistance and support. Last week's candle pierced with force the average of 50 sessions and exceeded the area of interest indicated but leaving a long upper wick, a sweep of liquidity that gives us a first sign that the liquidity sweep of this correction may be near and beginning its formation.

For the time being, the upward trend since April lows would remain active with highs and lows higher than before. Having said that, in our opinion we believe that the market is capitulating and a bottom may be forming, therefore we do not expect falls greater than 5%-10% (subtracting the support zone) in the short term to consolidate and gain momentum to recover the levels above the average of 50 sessions and continue with the upward movement.

In this article, which we did on the 2× leveraged ETF from the most fundamental on-chain point of view (useful for a medium/long term view) we comment on the good on-chain momentum of the Solana network but that the entry should be cautious and with a good risk management. And in one of the conversations, we gave our opinion that it was better to wait and watch to find confirmations of exhaustion in the corrective movement and volatility in the short term to look for better entry opportunities.

Conclusion

The managers of SSK are performing good operational management in terms of seeking passive returns on assets. The decision to use the Jito platform that corresponds to the best rated path in our analysis along with the safety and simplicity of securing 3% via CoinShares’ staked-SOL ETP (SLNC), sounds prudent and well-reasoned.

It should be remembered that the fund has no obligation to distribute these dividends and that these distributions come exclusively from staking rewards, which the fund converts to USD by selling a portion of SOL. However, if the price of SOL falls , the fund would have the same amount of currencies but with a lower valuation so to maintain the same dynamics in the volume of payments received, they would be obliged to sell more solanas, which reduces the NAV and part of the payment can materialize in the form of ROC.

The expense ratios comparative to other similar products (with yield dividends) are lower, they have a higher volume and being the same exposure, the attractiveness is remarkable.

As for Solana, we recently conducted an analysis of another ETF in which we valued the momentum of the cryptocurrency and our criterion has not yet changed: the macro uptrend remains intact, no signs of change have yet been given and we maintain the prospect of tactical buy in reversals + hold in a 5-6 month horizon. Currently, we’re in a pullback that can be used to add on weakness, that's why we recommend buying SSK as we see actual value as an opportunity to accumulate more SOL at a “discount” and no worries if price falls between another 5-10%, as it is normal to retest support areas and gain energy for the next impulse.

As always, manage risk when trading/investing. Thank you very much for your time.

data.title: Brinker International: Temporary Headwinds, Long-Term Upside
data.description:
I had last written about Brinker International (EAT) in May 2025 and found it a Buy on a turnaround story firing on both margin expansion and a topline growth in the face of macro pressures. I had noted the valuations were slightly tricky compared to its own historical averages at the time, but did not acknowledge the turnaround to the extent the topline-margin growth was shaping up.

This immediate valuation rerating was visible till July, when the shares rallied by ~30%. This jump was somewhat aided by a historical turnaround in the broader markets from the April lows, but outperformance against the S&P 500 vindicates the opportunity I had identified at the time.

Since the highs in July though, Brinker has undergone significant corrections, largely due to multiple contractions more than any material change in the growth or earnings story. This is broader macro concerns at play, particularly pressuring discretionary spending stocks like Brinker. That plus upcoming unfavorable comps as the turnaround plays out the majority of outperforming narrative over the next 1-2 quarters.

A Look at Valuations

A broad look at how the share prices have moved across consumer discretionary (proxied by IWC) since May shows that restaurants and eateries have been pressured more than the discretionary sector as a whole. Within the eatery basket, Brinker has been punished more severely.

Forward PE ratios in the casual dining and adjacent sectors have been pressured across the board too. In the case of Brinker, the ~32% valuation contraction is in fact extraordinary in comparison to peers and more than accounts for the ~22% drop in share prices since.

Brinker trades at a forward PE ratio of ~10.4x, heavily discounted now compared to it own 5 year average (31% lower than average) and the sector median (~16.34x sector median non-GAAP forward PE). This extreme discount is an opportunity as the fundamentals are not changed drastically. The comps have become tougher and are going to be challenging as well - but there is no structural worsening of the proposition. The valuation contraction is part sectoral pressure due to the macro uncertainties, and part discounted opportunity as Brinker has corrected more than its performance warrants.

The Margin Concerns are not Structural

Cost pressures have eased actually from May, with margins trending further upward, reaching healthy double digits in terms of TTM operating margins. The margin improvements have been steady since 2023 and there is no broader concern seen there.

Management highlighted a manageable repair and maintenance cost that supported margins. More importantly, structurally the pricing power will be tested going ahead with price increases coming into play in October and expectedly in January, to offset some of the higher costs due to tariffs on beef and shrimps. Additionally, the rest of the financial year could see margins stagnating due to Maggiano’s softer performance, timing of labor and advertising expenses.

quarter 2 is the one that's going to have the most pressure just because of, like I said, the timing of Chili's expenses and then just the impact of Maggiano's, specifically on that second quarter. Depending how fast Maggiano's can recover, I think it could have some incremental pressure in 3 and 4.

Maggiano’s impact on EPS could see a 6-8% erosion in Q2 EPS, with lower impacts in the subsequent quarters. Overall, the margins pressure, despite a decent run up in the past calls for some valuation compression. But, nothing discussed in Q1 FY2026 points to any permanent earnings issue. It’s more short-term noise than bad news, and the valuation discount largely reflects macro pressure and sectoral rotation, not a structural risk to the turnaround thesis.

Issues with Higher Comps

The sales story appears to be under pressure too after a blockbuster run up in 2025. Chili's sequential decline in Q1 FY2026 is real. But this reality ignores the run up from the past two years that has lifted the comps significantly. The comps are still healthy - Chili's is up ~21% YoY. But without continued growth, the comps may start looking less attractive. To put that point in perspective, even if Chili's growth were to stagnate and sales stay where they are for the next 3 quarters, it would be up by a decent ~15% compared to Q4 2024 (2 year comp growth). However, it would be down by ~7% YoY (because the high Q4 2025 highs). Valuations may temporarily punish this narrative, but fact is that sales will still remain healthy even if such stagnation shows up.

Chili's comps are a new term narrative hurdle for valuations. However, management remains confident of continuing the Chili's growth story.

So here's what we learned tracking monthly cohorts. For both new and regular guests, trip frequency is staying very stable regardless of the cohort. What this means is our restaurant experience is bringing guests back and retaining the traffic over time versus bringing them in once and having them not come back to Chili's. This data, along with our quarter-to-date sales and traffic trends, give us confidence we'll be able to roll over the Q2 plus 31.4% sales growth and the plus 19.9% traffic growth from prior year.

Maggiano’s deceleration is more structural though. Maggiano’s turnaround is being driven by a four-pillar plan focusing on menu classics, service efficiency, targeted maintenance and management engagement. Thankfully, its current underperformance only modestly affects total company sales, but a successful turnaround will help overall comps at a time when Chili's cools off on comps.

Why the Negatives are an Opportunity

Overall, valuations have declined due to macro uncertainties plus Brinker's own fundamentals looking tougher. On one hand we have a margin stagnation possibility in the upcoming quarters. At the same time, tougher comps are likely to make the topline look optically weaker.

What I see though is margins not affected structurally but more due to timing of labor and advertising expenses. The topline also is still looking supported and Chili's growth is expected to continue, although at lower levels and optically weaker comps. Nothing yet points to a ~33% valuation correction from May and an even steeper contraction from the July highs. The Maggiano drag is real, but that is already digested. The management is also focused on a strategic plan to get the operational gaps addressed there. Turnaround there will be widely watched even though it does not contribute material numbers to the headline metrics.

Despite all the drag, the guidance maintains net income per diluted share (excluding special items) at $10.2 for FY 2026 at the midpoint. That is still ~15% up from the $8.9 number we saw in 2025. Even extrapolating the Q4 FY2025 income per diluted share of $2.49, the 2026 earnings are likely to be 2-3% up from the prior year. Revenues are guided to be up by 5% in FY2026 too.

Earnings expectations have not been adjusted in Q1. The valuation drop in response to Q4 2025 and further after Q1 2026 , indicates markets pricing in a lot more negatives that fundamentals show. The key is that management has maintained net income per diluted share guidance despite these headwinds. Even conservatively extrapolating Q4 2025 results, the market is discounting more downside than fundamentals justify.

The disproportionate valuation drop has taken the forward PE ratio to levels last seen a couple of years ago. In the meantime, EPS has grown and 2026 is still a year of EPS growth despite the negative narrative.

The overall story of Brinker is one of unfavorable comp, temporary margin headwinds and a Maggiano drag that seems to be weighing on the fundamentals. The fundamentals are looking pressured, but should still manage a growth year for Brinker both in earnings and topline. However, valuations are discounting the absolute strength in light of the excellent turnaround seen in 2025. Brinker is still in a much better place compared to 2024, while valuations have rocked back to those levels. That presents a buying opportunity for patient investors. As the macro story stabilizes over the next year or so, and Brinker addresses some of the operational gaps (like in Maggiano), there could be decent upside to be reaped both due to earning surprises as well as valuation expansion.

data.title: The Google Enterprise Story Finally Feels Real
data.description:
An interesting thing hit me as I listened to a variety of Google (GOOG, GOOGL) executives making a slew of new business and enterprise-focused announcements at their Chrome Summit in NYC: I started to feel like I was at a Microsoft (MSFT) event. As strange as that may sound, I mean it in the best possible way for both companies. I’m used to hearing Microsoft tell a comprehensive, business-focused story that highlights not only important new functionality, but the inherent security and operational integration factors that enterprise IT departments depend on to bring new products and services into their environments.

In Google’s case, while they have been talking about and shipping enterprise-focused products and capabilities for several years, the company hasn’t always had the kind of comprehensive, business-focused perspective that IT professionals look for. Instead, it’s always felt a bit like a bunch of interesting technologies coming from several distinct groups delivered as a series of point products that are targeted at enterprise IT - but weren’t always a great fit.

However, at the Chrome Summit (an event that frankly would have been more accurately titled Google for Enterprise), Google presented a broad-reaching, unified story demonstrating that they really get what modern enterprise IT departments need. Under the theme of “Connected work experiences with AI at the core”, the company highlighted how it can bring together consistent experiences across multiple platforms and devices and use its Gemini AI capabilities to serve as the core connective tissue between them all.

Importantly, the company also highlighted an impressive range of new partnerships with big names in enterprise security and device trust/authentication, including companies like Cisco (CSCO), CrowdStrike (CRWD), Okta (OKTA), Omnissa, Zscaler (ZS) and more. This is critical because it reflects Google’s more open willingness to integrate their products and services into existing enterprise IT architectures as opposed to thinking that they could (or should) do it all on their own. While one could argue that new partnerships aren’t that big of a big deal, they definitely added to the sense that Google now has a more realistic and mature perspective on how to work their way into the enterprise.

What’s fascinating is that it all seems to have come together quickly and for several important reasons. First, the company has been on an absolute tear when it comes to releasing new products. From the Gemini Enterprise launch just a few weeks back to yesterday’s news (and even the Private Cloud Compute news from earlier this week), it’s clear that the company is working hard to maintain the frenetic pace of change that the AI revolution has brought to us. In addition, after many years of creating what were supposed to be a whole bunch of big important initiatives, only to be discontinued, have their names changed, etc., the company is finally demonstrating consistency with Gemini. Its success and its applicability to so many areas have served as a rallying cry to bring what seemed to be previously very disparate groups (and efforts) together. Finally, the success of Google Cloud in enterprise has also brought a new sense of respect and trust that arguably serves as a halo for all the company’s enterprise efforts.

In terms of capabilities, Google also spent a lot of time focusing on the very tactical benefits for enterprise users. In the Chrome browser, for example, the company talked about new security and threat detection capabilities, data loss prevention (DLP) tools, easier integration with SecOps tools and more. New Android Enterprise features include capabilities that make it easier for IT to create device trust solutions as well as manage phones, tablets and even new devices like the Samsung Galaxy XR headset, which was built in conjunction with Google.

Arguably the biggest news of the event was the announced “relaunch” of Cameyo, coming November 17. Cameyo, which Google purchased last year, provides the ability to run legacy Windows applications within a Chrome browser completely independent of the underlying operating system. It uses a form of Virtual Application Delivery (VAD) to essentially convert these legacy applications into Progressive Web Apps (PWAs) that run within the browser. Importantly, it can do this without needing to maintain a costly and confusing VDI infrastructure. Plus, Google took advantage of the advanced security capabilities now built into the Chrome Browser - for things like security protections and controls - as well as a Gemini AI overlay to bring more intelligence to these apps. The net result is a solution that not only lets companies much more easily deal with the huge load of legacy applications they still run, but do so in a more robust and potentially automatable way. It’s yet another example of the more down-to-earth, real-world problem-solving capabilities that Google demonstrated throughout the event.

Of course, it wouldn’t be a Google event if there weren't some focus on new devices and capabilities as well. To that end, the company talked up its new Pixel 10 phones and some of the agentic capabilities it’s bringing to them via Magic Cue, its predictive personal assistant capability. Because Magic Cue can - with your permission, of course - access things like your emails, calendars and other information on your Pixel 10 phone, it can smartly insert itself into text threads, help you find information in context and much more. The company also showed off the forthcoming Google Beam 3D video communication and virtual meeting system/display, which HP is expected to bring to market in March of next year. Finally, the company also teased some amazing new work that the DeepMind division is doing on new types of models and AI applications.

In the end, Google did a solid job of demonstrating how they can control every layer of an AI solution stack from semiconductors through cloud infrastructure to devices, operating systems, productivity applications, AI models and more. More than simply listing all these aspects, they also showed how they’re starting to integrate them together in ways that can offer meaningful benefits to IT professionals. It’s a story that finally feels like it’s coming to life.

Disclaimer: Some of the author's clients are vendors in the tech industry.

Disclosure: None.

Original Source: Author

data.title: Intel's Incredible Rally Might Be Over (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
My Thesis Update On INTC Stock

When you're an analyst, it's always nice to see when your predictions come to reality. After multiple bullish calls that I published on Intel Corporation (INTC) since 2024, the stock finally shot up, outperforming some of its main peers - like Nvidia (NVDA) - since mid-July 2025 (when I last covered INTC).

The move-up looks phenomenal - you rarely see INTC outpacing NVDA on a YTD basis, and beating the S&P 500 index (SP500) by a factor of over 5 at the same time.

But from what I see today, the current strength in sentiment around INTC and the subsequent rally seem to be running ahead of themselves. When the stock was deeply out of favor at the low $20s, nobody believed in its reversal story. Now, the influx of speculators/bullish investors, who entered after Intel's Altera sale to Silver Lake in mid-September and further increased following the company's Q3 earnings beat and new management's commentary, led to an overreaction. I believe that INTC's fair price, with all the existing risks, is below the current one. There's a lot for Intel to do on both the operational and financial fronts to prove that the turnaround is meaningfully changing the fundamental picture. I see at least some consolidation ahead, and that's why I'm downgrading my rating to Hold.

My Reasoning For A Downgrade

I expected previously that after a series of negative earnings consensus revisions, which logically came after Intel's few missing quarters, the company's Q2 was supposed to impress as the updated management team quickly moves on its initial cost-cutting and internal restructuring.

We're approaching Intel's Q2 FY2025 earnings print [7/24/2025, post-market], and my words regarding "too pessimistic assumptions" most relate to this upcoming earnings release, in my opinion. Also, the Street may be missing the likely shift in the management's narrative on the earnings call. So, I expect INTC's strong bullish reaction following Q2 results and earnings call, hence my "Buy" rating reiteration today.

Source: From my July update on INTC.

My call was one quarter earlier than it should have been - Intel beat only in Q3, not in Q2:

Their consolidated sales in Q3 went up by 6% QoQ to $13.7 billion, and thanks to the gross profit margin hitting 4 points above guidance (~40%), the resulting diluted EPS of positive 90 cents was incomparably better than last year's loss per share of -$3.88. The Q3 marked the first adjusted-EPS-positive quarter since late 2023.

The AI demand turned out to be strong even for CPUs, which are still critical for inference and edge workloads, so Intel's DCAI segment's revenue increased by 5% QoQ. It's not the largest segment for Intel, and it wasn't the fastest-growing one either (the CCG segment saw an 8% QoQ revenue growth thanks to the Windows 11 refresh and strong pricing mix). What was important in the data center segment is that the EBIT margin within DCAI expanded from 16.1% last quarter to 23.4%. This proved that Intel can sustainably turn a profit from its DCAI products going forward, so the positive post-earnings market reaction looks completely logical to me.

But I'd not buy INTC after its meteoric rise now because while its operational turnaround looks real, it is still fragile and incomplete. Their foundry business, where revenue fell by 4% QoQ, is still a major drag on profitability, and it will remain unprofitable in the next few years (at least).

They're trying to catch up for TSMC (TSM), but the volumes and, more importantly, customer trust are the things they can't build overnight. The foundry itself is a capital-intensive and margin-dilutive business with minimal chances for turning into anything successful in the next few years. Here, I fully agree with Citi's take on the deal between Nvidia and Intel, which was also a major catalyst fueling the latter's rally.

We expect minimal improvement for Intel as better graphics won't make Intel's CPU better than AMD's given the processor is the main performance driver,” Citi analyst Christopher Danely wrote in a note to clients. “In addition, we don't expect much from the AI product given the small [total addressable market] ($1 to $2 billion) and our belief that the stock is pricing in success in its leading-edging foundry business, which we believe has minimal chance to succeed.

Source: Seeking Alpha, Citi.

Intel's management itself cautioned on the call that 18A yields, while improving, won't reach industry-competitive levels until late 2026, and the ramp-up will be "lumpy" in nature, which spells like "lumpy" growth rates ahead. If they under-spent on growth in, say, Q3 or Q4, we shouldn't extrapolate that into the next year. And the same principle should work in the opposite direction.

Since 2015, INTC's revenues have fallen by 3.46%, and now the market expects that the firm's top-line will go up at a CAGR of ~9% over the next 4-5 years. The CAGR means it's an annual growth. So, the projections became too bullish, and I don't see enough hard data in hand to justify this long-term expectation just yet.

Intel's stock recovery was way quicker than I initially expected, and it eroded the valuation discount completely, in my opinion. Yes, on the surface, INTC is cheaper than Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) and NVDA in terms of EV/EBITDA (I don't take P/E intentionally, as INTC's profitability has only recently turned positive again):

But the peers above are already highly profitable, their margins are stable, and they're not viewed by the market as outsiders. All this is important to keep in mind when matching Intel's multiples with them.

Compared to the sector's medians, Intel is at best fairly valued right now, if the current EBITDA consensus for FY2025 is true.

The room for further expansion has shrunken significantly in recent weeks, and Intel needs to overdeliver versus the current already bullish estimates to fuel the current rally further. I simply doubt it can happen because after the low-hanging fruit is cut (the initial cost-cutting is over), the further they go in optimizing their OPEX and trying to boost top-line growth, the more difficult it becomes. The market, on the other hand, thinks the opposite is true - otherwise, we wouldn't have seen the growth tilt toward FY2027-2028:

In terms of technical analysis, INTC is right at the likely downward-sloping price channel, which coincides with the price level of $37-39/share. The distance to the 52-week moving average has expanded to over 35%, and the current steepness in the rally doesn't look sustainable to me. The odds for at least a healthy correction amid likely forward earnings reassessments ahead are their highest, in my opinion.

Final Thoughts

I know my reasoning above might sound more bearish rather than neutral, but in reality, I want Intel to succeed in its turnaround. And the first moves they made are looking good, and they were logical. What I don't like is the market's reaction to them. It might sound weird that I don't like to see so much growth in INTC after my previous Buy calls. I do like the rally. But I admit that it might have gone too far from what can be justified by Intel's fundamentals, which is a solid reason for me to downgrade to Hold. I expect some medium-term weaknesses, and if Intel stock dips by 20-25% from today's levels with nothing dramatic on the corporate side as a background, I'd be willing to upgrade to Buy again. Let me know in the comments below if you have another view on the current setup in INTC.

Good luck with your investments!

data.title: MSFU: Why This Could Be A Smart Long-Term Bet
data.description:
Not long ago, I briefly presented my bullish case on Microsoft (MSFT) in the context of the company's fiscal 1Q'26 results, which I will revisit below. While I think that owning the stock is a good idea for long-term investors, even at current prices and valuations that many consider rich, I also think that what is worth doing might be worth overdoing—with caution.

My more aggressive strategy on Microsoft involves trading the Direxion Daily MSFT Bull 2X Shares ETF (MSFU), a leveraged ETF that has produced 83% cumulative gains since its September 2022 inception for an annualized return of nearly 20%.

What is MSFU?

Very simply put, MSFU is a 2x leveraged play on the daily performance of Microsoft. If the underlying asset (Microsoft stock) is up 1% on the day, MSFU's target is to be up 2%. The same is true with an inverted signal: double the daily gains, but also double the daily losses.

The fund has about $250 million in assets under management and trades an average of 660,000 shares per day (about $32 million worth of MSFU), making it quite liquid despite its modest size. Last time I checked, the bid-ask spread was a narrow 4 cents on a share price of nearly $50, which reinforces the idea that this is a reasonably tradable security.

The risks of the leveraged strategy

Leveraged funds, whether single-stock or not, are the rare cases in which talking about the risks before the opportunities makes the most sense. I like to use the sharp knife metaphor: leverage can allow a skillful user of the tool to produce value that otherwise would be hard to find, but it can also cut deep into the flesh of those who use it irresponsibly.

MSFU has produced volatility of 38% and a maximum drawdown of 36% since its October 2022 inception. This is clearly not an ideal historical risk profile for conservative investors. Having said that, keep in mind that MSFU's volatility pales in comparison to that of Tesla (TSLA) at 63% and Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) at 57%.

The other key risk that I see is that single-stock funds do not offer any diversification. To be fair, Microsoft is not a stock known for outsized pullbacks. In the past ten years, the worst drawdown was -37%, which is painful but not catastrophic (we will discuss later how a strategy for trading MSFU would likely sidestep these large corrections). The bigger problem I see is that a single-day selloff of, say, 12%, driven by a disappointing earnings report, would wipe out about one-fourth of MSFU's value in a single trading session.

Lastly, there is volatility drag. Holding a leveraged position that resets every day over long periods of time means that the goal of producing twice the daily return of the underlying asset will not translate to twice the long-term gains. The more volatile the underlying asset is, the more the leveraged position will decline over time, even if the underlying asset trades sideways or modestly higher.

The table below shows the return of a 3x strategy at different levels of performance for the underlying asset and at different volatility assumptions. The 2x approach used by MSFU would not create the same level of volatility drag, but the table helps to illustrate the general idea.

The appeal of the underlying stock

If MSFU has a chance of producing sizable gains over time, it is imperative that Microsoft stock perform well—an obvious but important observation. I have written about Microsoft several times since I turned bullish on the company in 2018, the last article having come out recently.

At the core of my bullish thesis on MSFT are (1) the predictability of earnings made possible by the subscription-heavy business model, which helps to support the perception of lower risk by investors; and (2) a growth story that, rather than fizzling, seems to be picking up speed, as suggested by the chart below on the growth rate increase in Azure.

Valuation is a pushback that I hear often from bears and skeptics. At a high level, and as I mentioned to one of my readers recently, I think that Microsoft deserves to trade at a premium to non-tech peers that also feature relatively low volatility (MSFT has consistently been the least volatile stock of the Mag 7) and somewhat low correlations with the S&P 500 (MSFT's correlation has been 0.59 since the 1980s). I say "premium" because these low-vol, low-correlation peers tend to have substantially lower growth prospects compared to Microsoft: Walmart (WMT), Costco (COST), and Philip Morris (PM).

At a target P/E of 35x, I see MSFT trading at more than $650 per share next year, for 30%-plus upside over 12 months or less, once the stock is valued on its projected fiscal 2027 EPS of nearly $19. Multiply this gain by two and shave off a solid 10 percentage points for volatility drag (I am assuming 25%-30% volatility in Microsoft), and we might be looking at a 55% annual return from owning MSFU by 2026 in a great-case scenario.

The trading strategy

Having said the above, I don't think that "HODL'ing" (i.e., holding on to MSFU at all costs and against all odds) is a smart approach. The reason: a crumbling Microsoft stock could nearly wipe out the entire value of MSFU, as the daily losses get magnified by two and compile on top of each other.

First, I would not allocate more than a small portion of my portfolio to MSFU ("small" is open for interpretation, but 10% is probably too much even for aggressive investors). Second, I need to be comfortable with the following idea regarding risks: if I accept that day-to-day movements in MSFU can and likely will be large, I can do something about protection against deep drawdowns, and the reward will (or can) be outsized total returns.

"Doing something", in this case, is a simple cut-the-loss strategy. I have written quite a bit about using moving averages to limit the impact of a meaningful pullback: own shares only when the 14-day moving average of the underlying asset price is above the 200-day moving average, for example.

The chart above, in log scale, summarizes how these three different strategies would have performed in the past 10 years:

  • Buy and hold MSFT: 28% annualized return, Sharpe ratio of 0.96, assuming a 2% risk-free rate, and a maximum drawdown of -37%. An initial $100,000 in 2016 would have turned into $968,000 in 2025
  • Buy and hold MSFU: 52% annualized return, Sharpe ratio of 0.93, and a maximum drawdown of -65%. An initial $100,000 in 2016 would have turned into $4.8 million in 2025
  • Trade MSFU using moving averages: 48% annualized return, Sharpe ratio of 0.98, and a maximum drawdown of -53% (compliments of a vicious bear market that was triggered by the beginning of the COVID-19 crisis, and that the moving average strategy would have done little to protect against, given the very short timeframe of only 24 trading days between all-time highs and the largest drawdown in the period). An initial $100,000 in 2016 would have turned into $3.6 million in 2025

Clearly, buying and holding MSFU over the past decade would have been the better strategy, although only in absolute return terms. But, of course, past results are not indicative of future performance.

The more nuanced take is that using the moving average strategy allows the investor to turbo-charge returns on a high-quality play (assuming MSFT qualifies as such, which I think it does) while softening the landing when the market turns sour. Notice how Microsoft's terrible 2022, when the stock reached a maximum drawdown of -37%, would not have been much more damaging to the moving average strategy, which would have lost a maximum of -39%.

In summary

I believe that MSFT is an excellent stock to own. The more sophisticated, aggressive, active investor who is comfortable using leverage (and the tools that are likely needed to manage it, including broad portfolio diversification and stop-losses) might want to take a close look at MSFU. While I don't expect the leveraged single-stock ETF to outperform the underlying asset in risk-adjusted terms, it could lavishly beat it on an absolute basis.

Doing well on this and other similar trades could make the difference between having or not having an extra one or two million dollars in the portfolio many years down the road.

data.title: John Hancock 2055 Lifetime Blend Portfolio Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market review and outlook

Financial assets delivered strong returns in the quarter, with both stocks and bonds registering gains. A favorable backdrop of positive global growth, falling interest rates across the developed markets, and a lack of pronounced negative news fueled a surge in investors’ appetite for risk. Most notably, the U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) cut rates by a quarter point in September, and the markets anticipated that additional easing could be in store over the months ahead. Investors were further cheered by positive corporate earnings reports and the relatively limited initial economic impact from tariffs.

These developments helped propel major equity indexes to a series of all-time highs. The United States led the way higher behind renewed enthusiasm about the artificial intelligence theme and the technology sector more broadly. Small-cap stocks, which benefited from the improving interest rate outlook and investors’ preference for risk, performed particularly well. The emerging markets were another top performer, with China registering an impressive gain amid revived optimism about the country’s economic outlook.

The global fixed-income markets also produced positive returns, reflecting the increasingly favorable shift in Fed policy. Longer-term debt and credit-oriented categories generally provided the best results.

Contributors and detractors

Asset allocation contributed to the fund’s relative performance in the quarter. An overweight in equities and corresponding underweight in bonds were the primary contributors to third-quarter relative performance. An overweight in emerging-market bonds in the fixed-income portfolio was a further positive. An underweight in emerging-market stocks detracted, as did an overweight in U.S. mid caps.

Portfolio changes

Investment conditions remained broadly positive at the close of the period, highlighted by ongoing optimism related to economic conditions and global central bank policy. However, valuations have become fairly full across many areas of the equity and fixed-income markets. With this in mind, we maintain a diversified portfolio designed to hold up through a wide range of possible outcomes. We think it’s better to keep a long-term view rather than trying to chase returns in specific categories. We believe this approach should hold the fund in good stead over time, and we think its merits may become increasingly apparent if the market backdrop becomes less favorable than investors are anticipating.

data.title: Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Week 245: $24,500 Allocated, $2,722.19 In Projected Dividends
data.description:
It was a difficult week for the market as the Nasdaq fell -4.43% while the S&P 500 declined by -2.72%. There was a major tech retracement as some of the investment community started to question the valuations of some technology companies after negative economic data. As the government shutdown continues there is a delay in official reports and the market is now relying on private sector data as their economic indicators. A report from Challenger, Gray & Christmas showed that U.S. employers announced the largest number of job cuts for the month of October in over 20 years which sparked fears of a weakening labor market. We also got a report on Friday from the University of Michigan indicating that consumer sentiment had fallen sharply to its lowest level since June 2022. On Friday the government had been shut down for 38 days and there was rumors circulating that the Fed could be pushed into a situation where they wouldn’t cut in December if they didn’t have official data from the government to review. This is the first time in 245 weeks where I didn’t get my portfolio data finished on Sunday night so the portfolio numbers will reflect after Mondays increase. On Monday the market rallied as it looked like a deal to open the government was reached and the market had one of its best days of the year. I am super bullish on the rest of the year as I believe we’re heading into a strong business environment as the Fed lowers rates and we see productivity gains from A.I.

After the massive decline last week and the rally in the beginning of this week things are looking good for a year end rally after it looks like a deal has been reached to reopen the government. After allocating $24,500 to the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio, the account balance finished week 245 at $32,274.44. The account's profitability has increased by $616.15 (8.49%) to $7,874.44 which is a return on invested capital of 32.27%. This week I added to my positions in ONEOK (OKE) and Starwood Property Trust (STWD). The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $55 of dividend income in week 245. The combination of reinvesting the generated dividend income and making these investments increased the forward projected annualized dividend income by $15.24 (0.57%) to $2,722.19. The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio has generated $5,205.15 of income since its inception, which is 21.25% of the total capital I have allocated toward the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio. As the government reopens I think we will get a bit of a surge as more investors are likely to think a Santa Clause rally is going to occur especially since the Fed will have data to go off at the next FOMC meeting.

The Overall Performance of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio

After the rebound on Monday the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio is at all-time highs with an account value of $32,274.44 after investing $24,500. What’s crazy is that I am now generating $2,722.19 in forward dividend income which is a portfolio yield of 8.43% and a yield on cost of 11.11%. I am currently up 31.73% or $7,774.44 on invested capital. When I think about starting this account with $100, and adding $100 per week toward income producing assets I am quite pleased with the progress. My focus was to build a portfolio that would mitigate risk while generating recurring income. I wanted capital appreciation to be a secondary goal, as I was not focused on replicating or trying to exceed the market's return. I am excited to see where this goes as I think there is a good chance I will be generating around $2,850 in forward income heading into 2026.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Dividend Section

Here's how much dividend income is generated per investment basket:

  • ETFs $978.25 (35.94%)
  • Equities $604.60 (22.21%)
  • CEFs $441.82 (16.23%)
  • REITs $436.76 (16.04%)
  • BDCs $260.76 (9.58%)

Investors utilize dividends to supplement their income and live off them. I'm building a dividend portfolio for myself 30 years into the future. In 2022, I collected $507.80 in dividend income from 533 dividends. In 2023, I collected $978.11 in dividend income from 660 dividends. 2024 was a great year, as the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $1,623.14 in dividend income from 709 dividends. After 45 weeks in 2025, $2,025.47 of dividend income was produced, which is 124.79% of the total income from 2024. There were 682 positions that paid a dividend, which is 96.19% of the dividends generated in 2024.

There was $55 of dividend income generated this week, which brought the average amount of dividend income I have generated on a weekly basis to $45.01. This is an increase of $13.80 (44.20%) YoY, and there have been 4 weeks that exceeded $100, unlike in 2024, when the largest week was $89.13. I'm really excited for the rest of October to unfold, as the YoY data is showing me a huge trend going up and to the right with dividend income. Over the next several years, I should be able to exceed $5,000 in forward dividend income in this account and basically double my annualized allocation to the portfolio.

October ended up breaking my previous record of $209.73 in dividend income as I generated $229.93. We’re starting off November having generated $55 in the first week. I just looked at the calendar and because of when Thanksgiving falls I am not sure what the last week in November will look like. When I look at the chart below December was the second highest month last year and I have a feeling that I am going to exceed $225 in both November and December this year. The year is winding down and I really like the way the YoY progress looks on a monthly basis and I am excited for 2026 as I think there is a lot of opportunities in the market for income producing equities.

My long-term goal is to have 75% or more of the portfolio generate at least 1 share per year from their dividend income. There are currently 43 positions accomplishing this, which is emphasized by the green section of the table below. There is $590.11 of forward dividend income being generated from the new dividends being generated from all the positions generating at least 1 share from their dividends annually. Many positions are still lagging, but I am making a strong effort to allocate capital toward them so I can get at least 50% of the portfolio to accomplish this goal by the end of the year. My fixation on this is because it will allow me to advance the compounding impacts from the dividend snowball, and it also makes sure that I am not investing in the same positions each week.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Composition

ETFs are exceeding 20% of the portfolio again, and it's entirely my fault. I was also adding to REITs heading into the rate cut, which caused the portfolio to get a bit top-heavy. It's difficult for me to stay away from these sectors, especially after big tech crushed earnings. I am going to work on evening out the sectors and have them more proportional in the future. Individual equities now represent 38.45% of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio while generating 22.13% of the dividend income. REITs, ETFs, CEFs, and BDCs make up 61.55% of the portfolio and generate 77.87% of the forward income. I am working on getting individual equities to represent more of the portfolio. The long-term goal is to have the sectors be close to equal weight, but that may not happen for 5-10 years at this rate, if ever. I have been adding energy companies as I believe there is a big opportunity there, and I will probably continue allocating capital toward energy positions as time progresses.

Altria Group (MO) is starting to climb again as it now represents 4.06% of the portfolio. MO took a hit, but as more capital comes back into the market looking for yield I think that this Dividend King is going to become more attractive. I am happy with the way the portfolio is rounding out and as Verizon (VZ) starts to dip well below the 3% level I would consider adding more to my position. One of the keys to this portfolio's success has been the level of diversification. I am expecting the top 20 positions to change a bit next year, and I am hoping that I can get all the positions a bit more even in the future. While I have allowed myself a 5% maximum, I really want to have a well-rounded portfolio that can mitigate risk well.

None of the top-10 positions changed this week but some did pay dividends and I added to STWD. My allocation to the top 10 positions is $6,858.76, and they finished the week with a value of $9,031.67. This is an ROI of $2,172.91 (31.68%). There has been $1,427.31 of dividends generated and reinvested, which is 20.81% of the initial investment into these positions. I'm now projecting that $741.95 of dividend income will be generated from the top 10 holdings, which have a forward yield of 10.82%. These positions represent 27.98% of the portfolio, while their forward dividend income is projected to generate 27.26% of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio's annualized income.

Week 244 Additions

This week I added to ONEOK (OKE), and Starwood Property Trust (STWD).

ONEOK

  • Shares of OKE are down -36/10% over the past year which has pushed the dividend yield past 6%
  • I believe pipelines are going to get rerated as new companies can’t just come in and build new pipelines at a time when more energy is needed to fuel the data center buildout
  • OKE reaffirmed its 2025 adjusted EBITDA will come in between $8 billion to $8.45 billion on the earnings call and I believe this will continue to expand and allow OKE to increase the dividend in the future
  • As income comes back into the market looking for yield I think that OKE will attract new investors and we will see shares appreciate in value

Starwood Property Trust

  • Anytime STWD is trading at the point where the yield is over 10% I start adding to my position
  • STWD has been a long-time favorite of mine when it comes to REITs as its paid the same quarterly dividend for more than a decade
  • I believe that shares of STWD will do well in a lower rate environment as all of the investments they have made will position them for a bright future

Week 246 Gameplan

It may be time to add to Verizon again and I am also keeping my eye on the Cohen & Steers Infrastructure Fund (UTF).

Conclusion

We’re in the home stretch as the first week of November is in the books. We had a sharp decline then a miraculous reversal and the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio is now at all-time highs while producing $2,722.19 in forward dividend income. The S-curve is starting to occur and each month more capital gets plowed back into the portfolio. I am now reinvesting more than 50% of my monthly allocation from the dividends and I can’t wait until I am reinvesting more than 100% of my monthly allocations from the dividend income being produced. I am excited to see where I can take this portfolio as I am still very focused on building out this income stream over the coming years and before I know it I believe it will be producing over $5,000 in forward dividend income. Please leave all of your suggestions in the comment section and I enjoy corresponding with everyone.

data.title: NVR: 50-Year Mortgages To Aid Affordability Troubles?
data.description:
NVR, Inc. (NVR) is one of the U.S.’s largest homebuilders, focusing on the Mid-Atlantic, Southeast, and other regions. The company’s financials largely reflect ongoing trends in the housing market, now potentially facing a tailwind ahead – the Trump administration is considering 50-year mortgages, having noteworthy earnings implications for homebuilders. NVR’s recent results still highlight ongoing affordability concerns in the housing market.

Long-term housing market tailwinds have driven significantly good capital returns for most homebuilders. NVR’s long-term stock performance also reflects the fact, returning at a 15.9% CAGR over the past decade. NVR’s earnings growth and aggressive share buybacks have driven such high returns.

50-Year Mortgages: Aid to Homebuilders’ Earnings Slump?

The housing market could see a significant change ahead. The Trump administration is told to be working on introducing 50-year mortgages, introduced in a proposal by Federal Housing Finance Agency Director Bill Pulte. The addition would add a significantly longer loan term option to the current common 30-year and 15-year mortgage options.

The new mortgage term could boost housing demand; a longer loan term results in lower monthly payments, making housing more affordable for prospective homebuyers. The change would be notable especially in the current macroeconomic context - housing demand has been dampened by very weak housing affordability in the past couple of years. Housing affordability plummeted during the Covid pandemic with increasing interest rates and surging home prices, and hasn’t recovered so far. New home sales have mostly remained subdued as a result.

For homebuilders like NVR, PulteGroup (PHM), Lennar Corporation (LEN), D.R. Horton (DHI) and others, the 50-year mortgage could provide a considerable short- to mid-term boost to earnings. The longer loan term would very likely increase homebuyer demand noticeably, in turn raising NVR’s and others’ home closing volumes. The boost in demand could also indirectly cause housing prices to rise as housing supply is slow to react to higher demand; homebuilders’ margins could benefit from the higher-term mortgage. Over the long term, the positive earnings effects of the 50-year mortgage should still smooth out as industry dynamics adapt.

Ultimately, the proposal is still in its early stages – Bill Pulte has noted the 50-year mortgage to only be “a potential weapon in a wide arsenal of solutions”. The 50-year mortgage would still need the appropriate legal and regulatory changes. The administration is also considering other measures to aid the housing market, which could have similar effects. For now, the proposal is still a clear positive for homebuilders.

NVR’s Financials Reflect Industry Trends

NVR’s historical and current financial performance mainly reflects housing market dynamics. Over the long term, the company’s homebuilding revenues have grown at a 7.5% CAGR from 2015 forward and the operating margin has simultaneously improved from 12.1% to 17.9%. A longstanding housing undersupply has enabled NVR to have significant pricing power as housing prices have surged, raising the current return on capital to a great 22.9% level. Great capital returns on NVR’s growth have enabled the stock’s significant returns alongside other homebuilders.

After an overall positive earnings performance, NVR’s shorter-term performance reflects weak housing affordability. As I already noted, higher mortgage rates and other factors have caused subdued new home sales. Despite some moderation in rates in recent months, mortgage rates remain considerably high.

NVR’s Q3 results reflect the fact. Homebuilding revenues declined by -4% to $2.56 billion, reflecting a -5% decrease in home closings and a very slight year-on-year average selling price increase. Increased lot costs and limited pricing power led NVR’s gross margin to decline by 240 basis points to 21.0%, leading net income to plummet by -20% to $343 million. New orders declined by -16% to 4735 units in the quarter, still setting up especially weak expectations for the next couple of quarters.

The Q3 report underlines that the housing market still seems to be waiting for improved affordability. While countrywide home sales surged in August as mortgage rates moderated, the Q3 earnings season has again moderated forward expectations.

NVR’s Valuation Is Fair

I built a discounted cash flow (DCF) model for NVR. The DCF model estimates NVR’s 2026 to remain challenging due to a weak backlog, but that the housing market eventually provides grounds for a small 2027-2028 revenue rebound. The rebound assumes improved affordability through lower mortgage rates and the potential for a demand boost from the 50-year mortgage proposal. I ultimately estimate growth to stabilize at 2.5%, mainly assuming modest growth in housing prices.

I also assume margins to rebound slightly alongside the housing market; I assume NVR’s EBIT margin to stabilize at 18%, slightly lower than the company has achieved through the 2021-2024 period. The estimated amount of growth shouldn’t require significant additional real estate investments from NVR, making the company’s after-tax cash flow conversion quite good in the DCF model.

The estimates put NVR’s fair value at $6960, near the stock price at the time of writing. I believe that NVR’s stock is priced with fair earnings and cash flow projections, not providing asymmetric upside or downside for now. The focus is on the housing market, and whether the 50-year mortgage or other proposals will improve housing affordability to boost demand.

NVR’s stock trades at a 10.6 EV/EBIT multiple, slightly above the largest five homebuilders’ average EV/EBIT of 8.5. While in my opinion justified by NVR’s highest capital returns of the group, I don’t believe that the multiple gives way for more upside in the valuation.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 9.09%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I don’t expect NVR to leverage a noteworthy amount of debt over the long term, as the company’s debt position is currently very moderate. For cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.12% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.62% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent July estimate for the US. Yahoo Finance estimates NVR’s beta at 1.01. With a liquidity premium of 0.3%, the cost of equity and WACC both stand at 9.09%.

Takeaway

The housing market could see a boost ahead, if the Trump administration’s 50-year mortgage proposal or other proposals go through. Poor housing affordability continues to trouble homebuilders, reflected in NVR’s weak Q3 financials and new orders – affordability improvements are needed for homebuilders to recover into their historically very great earnings growth. NVR stock is valued fairly for now, and as such, I initiate the stock at a Hold rating.

data.title: With All Eyes On A Merger Delay, Getty Images Beats Earnings Yet Again
data.description:
I last wrote about Getty Images (GETY) in June, after Shutterstock (SSTK) shareholders approved the companies’ merger. It was also a time of some challenges for Getty, who had just missed Q1 EPS estimates on a combination of forex charges and weakness from the Palisades fire.

I rated them a buy because, while the stock was largely unchanged, there were some encouraging signs for an “off” year, that is to say, a year in which there is neither a major US election nor an Olympics to fuel editorial image demand. Despite that obvious drag, Getty was able to keep revenue more or less flat in Q1.

Here we are a few months later, and we have much to talk about. Obviously the status of the Shutterstock merger, which is likely postponed into 2026, is something to discuss, but more interesting immediate topic is the earnings, and we have two quarters to look at now.

Q2 and Q3: Encouraging Resilience for Off-Year Revenue

While forex charges continued to be a challenge in GAAP earnings in Q2, Getty was able to come in solidly ahead of earnings estimates, at least on a non-GAAP basis.

While obviously the forex loss overshadowed everything else in Q2, but for that, Getty had a very impressive result. Again, it’s an off year, and Getty not only managed to be in line with the previous year but also to slightly exceed it. If we normalize for currency losses, Getty would’ve actually been impressively profitable in Q2, especially for a year that lacks the traditional news stories that historically drove revenue growth. This became even more apparent with Q3.

Revenue in the case of Q3 was only in line with the previous year, but it’s even more impressive here because Q3 of 2024 included the 2024 Paris Olympics, a huge driver of revenue. Interesting also is that this time the forex loss was on the 2024 side, underscoring just how much it matters to GAAP earnings.

Getty’s Q3 revenue did decline on the editorial side of things, because without an Olympic Games and without a major US election cycle, it obviously would. Yet the creative side grew so much year over year it more or less made up for it, reflecting that Getty is growing beyond their heavy reliance on the news cycle to drive positive earnings.

The reasons the creative side growth is so strong take multiple forms. We’re seeing continued uptake in Getty’s generative AI offerings, though as they noted in the most recent conference call, it’s too soon to totally project how that will continue into the future. They’re conservative in their reckoning of it, but I think it’s relatively safe to say that their dalliance with generative AI is at the very least not a failure.

We’re also seeing improvement in their business with Hollywood, though they’re still not back to where they were before the 2023 strike. Here too, there is room for improvement.

Finally, we have the ongoing shift to more of a focus on subscription-based business, which continues to outperform the old model. Getty’s library is vast, and the value of having a subscription with such a company is obvious. There’s another avenue for growth that we’ve yet to see on the top or bottom line, though, and that’s also AI-related.

The Perplexity Deal

At the end of October, Getty announced a multi-year licensing deal with Perplexity across their AI-powered search efforts. Perplexity is pushing for a big share of the market with their new AI-enabled browser, Comet, and their deal with PayPal in September to give PayPal customers free trials of the Perplexity Pro service should give them a kickstart in entering the browser market in a meaningful way.

What Perplexity has done is exciting many, and now Getty has a vested interest in seeing that succeed, with integration of Getty’s API technology into Perplexity’s services. This improves Perplexity’s visual offerings and ensures attribution and origin links for Getty-owned images when they’re on display in Perplexity’s environment.

How much this will add to Getty’s revenue and how soon we’ll start to see such results are anyone’s guess, but here again it’s an encouraging sign that Getty is finding a way to make itself even more ubiquitous within an AI-focused world.

The deal helps Perplexity, and Getty should benefit from that, and it also creates a working relationship with an important up-and-coming AI brand, which could set the stage for even larger deals in the future.

But naturally, everyone following Getty’s stock has been focused on the merger with Shutterstock, so I’d be remiss if I didn’t go over where that stands.

A Merger Deferred, But Not a Merger Denied

The expectation was that Getty’s merger with Shutterstock would close by year’s end. That was potentially the most bullish aspect of this company, and of Shutterstock as well, because combined, the company would be even more of a force to be reckoned with.

The October 20 deadline for the UK’s Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) to rule on the merger saw both Getty's and Shutterstock’s stocks climb, coming as it did with little word out of the CMA. On the final day, though, the CMA extended the review of the merger to phase 2, and both stocks dropped on the news.

This is not, however, a rejection of the merger but simply an extension of the review, which both Getty and Shutterstock believe is not going to lead to a denial. It will, however, push the merger’s finalization into 2026.

That’s an annoyance but not necessarily a big problem, because unlike a lot of companies in a pending merger, neither Getty nor Shutterstock rested on their laurels waiting for the finalization but continued to grow their respective businesses, which should be all the stronger if and when the deal is finalized.

That’s important, because in the relatively unlikely case that something prevents the merger, neither company is going to be in a disastrous shape without the other. The more likely outcome, though, is still that the merger gets approved with some concessions and divestments, which the companies have already offered to make.

In my view this merger still happens; it just takes a little more time.

A Buy for the Patient Investor

So in the end, I’m going to reiterate my buy rating on Getty. I still believe that the arbitrage potential makes buying Shutterstock the more efficient way to play this merger, but Getty continues to progress as a company on its own, and the combined company should benefit greatly from the merger, but even without it, Getty is not a bad deal at this price.

Investors should worry less about the merger and keep an eye on how Getty is able to grow through their generative AI and also how the Perplexity deal starts to take shape as another avenue to get the company more consumer-facing.

In my view, the main risk here is not that the merger gets torpedoed, though that would be a near-term bearish event, but that the generative AI they’ve advanced doesn’t do as well as early indications are.

Getty’s progress as its own company is the important thing to focus on right now, as they continue to work on resolving the concerns of the CMA and finalizing the merger somewhere down the line.
